Die Verwendung von s.substringAfter(x) macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den substring-Aufruf durch substringAfter.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(s: String) {
      s.substring(s.indexOf('x'))
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(s: String) {
      s.substringAfter('x')
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceSubstringWithSubstringAfter

Meldet assert-Aufrufe, die prüfen, ob der Wert einer deklarierten Variable nicht null ist.

Die Verwendung von !! oder ?: kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt assert in Variablen-Initialisierungen durch den !!- oder ?:-Operator.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(p: Array<String?>) {
      val v = p[0]
      assert(v != null, { "Should be not null" })
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(p: Array<String?>) {
      val v = p[0] ?: error("Should be not null")
  }

Inspection ID: SimplifyAssertNotNull

Meldet until, das durch den Operator ..< ersetzt werden kann.

Jede Ersetzung von until durch ..< ändert die Semantik in keiner Weise.

UX-Untersuchungen haben gezeigt, dass Entwickler beim Lesen von Code, der ..< enthält, etwa 20–30 % weniger Fehler machen als bei until.

Beispiel:


  fun main(args: Array<String>) {
      for (index in 0 until args.size) {
          println(index)
      }
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun main(args: Array<String>) {
      for (index in 0..<args.size) {
          println(index)
      }
  }

Diese Inspektion wird nur gemeldet, wenn das Kotlin-Sprachlevel des Projekts oder Moduls 1.7 oder höher ist.

Inspection ID: ReplaceUntilWithRangeUntil

Meldet Top-Level-val-Eigenschaften in Objekten, die für eine bessere Leistung und Java-Interoperabilität als const deklariert werden könnten.

Beispiel:


  object A {
      val foo = 1
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  object A {
      const val foo = 1
  }
Für Bibliotheksautoren: Die Verwendung von const für öffentliche API-Symbole ist gefährlich, da unterschiedliche Werte verwendet werden könnten, wenn die Kompilierzeitversion der Bibliothek von der Laufzeitversion abweicht. Mit Vorsicht verwenden.

Inspection ID: MayBeConstant

Meldet if-Ausdrücke, die unmittelbar nach der Initialisierung prüfen, ob eine Variable null ist, und die in einen Elvis-Operator innerhalb des Initialisierers umgewandelt werden können.

Beispiel:


  fun test(foo: Int?, bar: Int): Int {
      var i = foo
      if (i == null) {
          return bar
      }
      return i
  }

Der Quick-Fix wandelt den if-Ausdruck mit dem Initialisierer in einen Elvis-Ausdruck um:


  fun test(foo: Int?, bar: Int): Int {
      var i = foo ?: return bar
      return i
  }

Inspection ID: FoldInitializerAndIfToElvis

Meldet for-Schleifen mit einer manuell inkrementierten Indexvariablen.

Eine for-Schleife mit einer manuell inkrementierten Indexvariablen kann mithilfe der Funktion withIndex() vereinfacht werden.

Der Quick-Fix "withIndex() anstelle der manuellen Index-Inkrementierung verwenden" kann zur automatischen Korrektur des Codes verwendet werden.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(list: List<String>): Int? {
      var index = 0
      for (s in list) { // <== kann vereinfacht werden
          val x = s.length * index
          index++
          if (x > 0) return x
      }
      return null
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(list: List<String>): Int? {
      for ((index, s) in list.withIndex()) {
          val x = s.length * index
          if (x > 0) return x
      }
      return null
  }

Inspection ID: UseWithIndex

Meldet if-then-Ausdrücke, die zu einem Elvis-Ausdruck (?:) zusammengefasst werden können.

Beispiel:


  fun maybeFoo(): String? = "foo"

  var foo = maybeFoo()
  val bar = if (foo == null) "hello" else foo

Der Quick-Fix konvertiert den if-then-Ausdruck in einen Elvis-Ausdruck (?:):


  fun maybeFoo(): String? = "foo"

  var foo = maybeFoo()
  val bar = foo ?: "hello"

Inspection ID: IfThenToElvis

Meldet Lambda-Ausdrücke in runden Klammern, die nach außen verschoben werden können.

Beispiel:


fun square(a: Int, b: (Int) -> Int) {
  b(a * a)
}

fun foo() {
  square(2, { it })
}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


fun foo() {
  square(2){ it }
}

Inspection ID: MoveLambdaOutsideParentheses

inspection.displayName: 'map.put()' kann in eine Zuweisung umgewandelt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet map.put-Funktionsaufrufe, die durch den Indizierungsoperator ([]) ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung von syntaktischem Zucker kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den put-Aufruf durch eine Zuweisung.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(map: MutableMap<Int, String>) {
      map.put(42, "foo")
  }

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(map: MutableMap<Int, String>) {
      map[42] = "foo"
  }

Inspection ID: ReplacePutWithAssignment

Meldet Aufrufargumente vom Typ Boolean, für die kein expliziter Parametername angegeben wurde.

Wenn mehrere Boolean-Literale nacheinander übergeben werden, kann die Reihenfolge der Parameter leicht vergessen werden, was zu Fehlern führen kann. Explizite Parameternamen erleichtern das Lesen und das Verständnis des Codes.

Beispiel:


  fun check(checkName: Boolean, checkAddress: Boolean, checkPhone: Boolean) {}

  fun usage() {
      check(true, false, true) // Was bedeutet das?
  }

Der Quick-Fix fügt die fehlenden Parameternamen hinzu:


  fun check(checkName: Boolean, checkAddress: Boolean, checkPhone: Boolean) {}

  fun usage() {
      check(checkName = true, checkAddress = false, checkPhone = true)
  }

Inspection ID: BooleanLiteralArgument

Meldet explizite Aufrufe der Funktionen get oder set, die durch den Index-Operator [] ersetzt werden können.

Kotlin ermöglicht benutzerdefinierte Implementierungen für einen vordefinierten Satz von Operatoren für Typen. Um einen Operator zu überladen, können Sie die entsprechende Funktion mit dem Modifikator operator kennzeichnen: 


  operator fun get(index: Int) {}
  operator fun set(index: Int, value: Int) {}
Die obigen Funktionen entsprechen dem Index-Operator.

Beispiel:

  class Test {
      operator fun get(i: Int): Int = 0
  }

  fun test() {
      Test().get(0) // Ersetzbares 'get()'
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:

  class Test {
      operator fun get(i: Int): Int = 0
  }

  fun test() {
      Test()[0]
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceGetOrSet

inspection.displayName: Lokales 'var' wird nie geändert und kann als 'val' deklariert werden

group.inspection:
Meldet lokale Variablen, die mit dem Schlüsselwort var deklariert, aber nie geändert wurden.

Kotlin empfiehlt, faktisch unveränderliche Variablen mit dem Schlüsselwort val zu deklarieren, um sicherzustellen, dass sich deren Werte niemals ändern.

Beispiel:


  fun example() {
      var primeNumbers = listOf(1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13)
      var fibonacciNumbers = listOf(1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13)
      print("Same numbers: " + primeNumbers.intersect(fibonacciNumbers))
  }

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt das Schlüsselwort var durch val:


  fun example() {
      val primeNumbers = listOf(1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13)
      val fibonacciNumbers = listOf(1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13)
      print("Same numbers: " + primeNumbers.intersect(fibonacciNumbers))
  }

Inspection ID: CanBeVal

Meldet map.get()!!, das durch map.getValue(), map.getOrElse() usw. ersetzt werden kann.

Beispiel:


fun test(map: Map<Int, String>): String = map.get(0)!!

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


fun test(map: Map<Int, String>): String = map.getValue(0)

Inspection ID: MapGetWithNotNullAssertionOperator

inspection.displayName: Der Aufruf der Java-Methode Map.forEach sollte durch Kotlins forEach ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Aufrufe von Non-Null-Assertionen (!!), die durch den Elvis-Operator und return (?: return) ersetzt werden können.

Non-Null-Assertionen können zu unerwarteten NPEs (NullPointerException) führen. Es gilt als bewährte Methode, die Verwendung von !! zu vermeiden.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die Non-Null-Assertion durch return oder return null.

Beispiel:


  fun test(number: Int?) {
      val x = number!!
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun test(number: Int?) {
      val x = number ?: return
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceNotNullAssertionWithElvisReturn

Meldet Aufrufe wie "abc".substring(0, 1), die durch "abc"[0] ersetzt werden können.

Das Abrufen eines Elements über den Index kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den substring-Aufruf durch einen Index-Operator.

Beispiel:


  fun foo() {
      "abc".substring(0, 1)
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun foo() {
      "abc"[0]
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceSubstringWithIndexingOperation

inspection.displayName: Sicherer Cast mit 'return' sollte durch eine 'if'-Typprüfung ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet sichere Casts mit return, die durch eine if-Typprüfung ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung der entsprechenden Funktion kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den sicheren Cast durch eine if-Typprüfung.

Beispiel:


  fun test(x: Any) {
      x as? String ?: return
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun test(x: Any) {
      if (x !is String) return
  }

Ebenso werden unsichere Casts der Form


    fun foo(o: Any) {
    o as String ?: return
}

erkannt und in eine Typprüfung konvertiert:


    fun foo(o: Any) {
    if (o !is String) return
}

Inspection ID: SafeCastWithReturn

Ersetzt den contains-Member-Aufruf durch den in-Operator.

Beispiel:

a.contains(b)

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:

b in a

Inspection ID: ReplaceContains

inspection.displayName: Guard-Clause kann durch Kotlin-Funktionsaufruf ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Guard-Klauseln, die durch einen Funktionsaufruf ersetzt werden können.

Beispiel:

  fun test(foo: Int?) {
      if (foo == null) throw IllegalArgumentException("foo") // Ersetzbare Klausel
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:

  fun test(foo: Int?) {
      checkNotNull(foo)
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceGuardClauseWithFunctionCall

inspection.displayName: Unsicherer Cast mit 'return' sollte durch einen sicheren Cast ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet unsichere Casts mit return in Variablenzuweisungen oder Wertargumenten, die durch einen sicheren Cast ersetzt werden können.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den unsicheren Cast durch einen sicheren Cast.

Beispiel


    fun test(x: Any): Any? {
        val y = x as String ?: return null
    }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun test(x: Any): Any? {
      val y = x as? String ?: return null
  }

Inspection ID: UnsafeCastWithReturn

Meldet Typparameter, die eine in- oder out-Varianz aufweisen können.

Die Verwendung von in- und out-Varianz ermöglicht eine präzisere Typinferenz sowie eine klarere Codesemantik in Kotlin.

Beispiel:


  class Box<T>(val obj: T)

  fun consumeString(box: Box<String>) {}
  fun consumeCharSequence(box: Box<CharSequence>) {}

  fun usage(box: Box<String>) {
      consumeString(box)
      consumeCharSequence(box) // Kompilierungsfehler
  }

Der Quick-Fix fügt den entsprechenden Varianz-Modifikator hinzu:


  class Box<out T>(val obj: T)

  fun consumeString(box: Box<String>) {}
  fun consumeCharSequence(box: Box<CharSequence>) {}

  fun usage(box: Box<String>) {
      consumeString(box)
      consumeCharSequence(box) // OK
  }

Inspection ID: AddVarianceModifier

Meldet ein redundantes else in einem if mit return.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(arg: Boolean): Int {
      if (arg) return 0
      else { // dieses else ist redundant, der Code in den geschweiften Klammern kann nach links verschoben werden
          someCode()
      }
  }

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun foo(arg: Boolean): Int {
      if (arg) return 0
      someCode()
  }

Inspection ID: RedundantElseInIf

inspection.displayName: Bereich kann in Indizes oder Iteration konvertiert werden

group.inspection:
Meldet until- und rangeTo-Operatoren, die durch Collection.indices oder eine Iteration über die Collection innerhalb einer for-Schleife ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung von Syntactic Sugar kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den manuell definierten Bereich durch das entsprechende Konstrukt.

Beispiel:


  fun main(args: Array<String>) {
      for (index in 0..args.size - 1) {
          println(args[index])
      }
  }

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun main(args: Array<String>) {
      for (element in args) {
          println(element)
      }
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceManualRangeWithIndicesCalls

Meldet String.format-Aufrufe, die durch String-Templates ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung von String-Templates macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den Aufruf durch ein String-Template.

Beispiel:


  fun main() {
      val id = "abc"
      val date = "123"
      val s = String.format("%s_%s_%s", id, date, id)
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun main() {
      val id = "abc"
      val date = "123"
      val s = "${id}_${date}_$id"
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceStringFormatWithLiteral

inspection.displayName: Zwei Vergleiche sollten in eine Bereichsprüfung umgewandelt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet zwei aufeinanderfolgende Vergleiche, die in eine Bereichsprüfung umgewandelt werden können.

Die Prüfung gegen einen Bereich macht den Code einfacher, da die Wiederholung des zu prüfenden Ausdrucks vermieden wird.

Beispiel:


  fun checkMonth(month: Int): Boolean {
      return month >= 1 && month <= 12
  }

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die vergleichsbasierte Prüfung durch eine Bereichsprüfung:


  fun checkMonth(month: Int): Boolean {
      return month in 1..12
  }

Inspection ID: ConvertTwoComparisonsToRangeCheck

inspection.displayName: Sollte durch 'equals(…, ignoreCase = true)' ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Vergleiche ohne Beachtung der Groß-/Kleinschreibung, die durch equals(..., ignoreCase = true) ersetzt werden können.

Durch die Verwendung von equals() müssen keine zusätzlichen Strings mit toLowerCase() oder toUpperCase() allokiert werden, um Strings zu vergleichen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt Vergleiche ohne Beachtung der Groß-/Kleinschreibung, die toLowerCase() oder toUpperCase() verwenden, durch equals(..., ignoreCase = true).

Hinweis: Dies kann die Semantik für bestimmte Locales ändern.

Beispiel:


  fun main() {
      val a = "KoTliN"
      val b = "KOTLIN"
      println(a.toLowerCase() == b.toLowerCase())
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun main() {
      val a = "KoTliN"
      val b = "KOTLIN"
      println(a.equals(b, ignoreCase = true))
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceWithIgnoreCaseEquals

Meldet Variablendeklarationen, die in einen when-Ausdruck verschoben werden können.

Beispiel:


fun someCalc(x: Int) = x * 42

fun foo(x: Int): Int {
  val a = someCalc(x)
  return when (a) {
    1 -> a
    2 -> 2 * a
    else -> 24
  }
}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


fun foo(x: Int): Int {
  return when (val a = someCalc(x)) {
    1 -> a
    2 -> 2 * a
    else -> 24
  }
}

Inspection ID: MoveVariableDeclarationIntoWhen

inspection.displayName: Accessor-Aufruf, der durch Property-Zugriffssyntax ersetzt werden kann

group.inspection:
Meldet Aufrufe von Java-get- und set-Methoden, die durch synthetische Kotlin-Properties ersetzt werden können.

Der Quick-Fix Property-Zugriffssyntax verwenden kann zur automatischen Korrektur des Codes verwendet werden.

Beispiel:


  // Java:
  public class JavaClassWithGetter {
      private final String expr = "result";

      // ...

      public String getExpr() {
          return expr;
      }
  }


  // Kotlin:
  fun test(j: JavaClassWithGetter) {
      // ...
      j.getExpr() // <== Der Quick-Fix vereinfacht den Ausdruck zu 'j.expr'
  }

Inspection ID: UsePropertyAccessSyntax

inspection.displayName: 'map.getOrDefault()' kann durch Indizierung und den Elvis-Operator ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Ersetzt den Funktionsaufruf map.getOrDefault(key, defaultValue) durch den Indexzugriff und den Elvis-Operator (map[key] ?: defaultValue).

Beispiel:


  fun test(map: Map<Int, String>) {
      map.getOrDefault(1, "foo")
  }


  fun test(map: Map<Int, String>) {
      map[1] ?: "foo"
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceMapGetOrDefault

Meldet if-then-Ausdrücke, die in einen Safe-Access-Ausdruck (?.) umgewandelt werden können.

Beispiel:


  fun bar(x: String) = ""

  fun foo(a: String?) {
     if (a != null) bar(a) else null
  }

Der Quick-Fix wandelt den if-then-Ausdruck in einen Safe-Access-Ausdruck (?.) um:


  fun bar(x: String) = ""

  fun foo(a: String?) {
     a?.let { bar(it) }
  }

Inspection ID: IfThenToSafeAccess

inspection.displayName: Objektliteral kann in Lambda umgewandelt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet anonyme Objektliterale, die ein Java-Interface mit einer einzelnen abstract-Methode implementieren und in einen Aufruf mit einem Lambda-Ausdruck umgewandelt werden können.

Beispiel:


class SomeService {
  val threadPool = Executors.newCachedThreadPool()
    
  fun foo() {
    threadPool.submit(object : Runnable {
      override fun run() {
        println("hello")
      }
    })
  }
}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun foo() {
    threadPool.submit { println("hello") }
  }

Inspection ID: ObjectLiteralToLambda

Meldet nicht idiomatische is-Typprüfungen für Objekte.

Es wird empfohlen, solche Prüfungen durch einen Referenzvergleich zu ersetzen.

Beispiel:


  object Foo

  fun foo(arg: Any) = when {
      arg is Foo -> ...
      arg !is Foo -> ...
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  object Foo

  fun foo(arg: Any) = when {
      arg === Foo -> ...
      arg !== Foo -> ...
  }

Inspection ID: RedundantObjectTypeCheck

inspection.displayName: 'copy'-Methode einer Datenklasse wird ohne benannte Argumente aufgerufen

group.inspection:
Meldet Aufrufe der Methode copy() von Datenklassen ohne benannte Argumente.

Da alle Argumente der copy()-Funktion optional sind, kann es schwierig sein zu verstehen, welche Eigenschaften geändert werden. Das explizite Angeben von Parameternamen macht den Code leicht verständlich, ohne dass zur Deklaration der data class navigiert werden muss.

Beispiel:


  data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)

  fun copyUser(user: User): User {
      return user.copy("John")
  }

Der Quick-Fix stellt Parameternamen für alle copy()-Argumente bereit:


  data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)

  fun copyUser(user: User): User {
      return user.copy(name = "John")
  }

Inspection ID: CopyWithoutNamedArguments

inspection.displayName: Zuweisung kann durch Verbundzuweisung ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Variablenänderungen mit einfachen Zuweisungen (z. B. y = y + x), die durch einen Zuweisungsoperator ersetzt werden können.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die Zuweisung durch einen Zuweisungsoperator.

Beispiel:


  fun foo() {
      val list = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
      list = list + 4
  }

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo() {
      val list = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
      list += 4
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceWithOperatorAssignment

Converts a range check (in) to two consecutive comparisons.

Example:


fun foo(arg: Int) = arg in 1..4

After applying quick-fix:


fun foo(arg: Int) = 1 <= arg && arg <= 4

Inspection ID: ConvertRangeCheckToTwoComparisons

Meldet Aufrufe von readLine(), die durch readln() oder readlnOrNull() ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung der entsprechenden Funktionen vereinfacht den Code.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt readLine()!! durch readln() und readLine() durch readlnOrNull().

Beispiel:


    val x = readLine()!!
    val y = readLine()?.length

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


    val x = readln()
    val y = readlnOrNull()?.length

Inspection ID: ReplaceReadLineWithReadln

Meldet if-Anweisungen mit drei oder mehr Zweigen, die durch einen when-Ausdruck mit Subjekt ersetzt werden können.

Beispiel:


  fun translateNumber(n: Int): String {
    return if (n == 1) {
      "one"
    } else if (n == 2) {
      "two"
    } else {
      "???"
    }
  }

Der Quick-Fix konvertiert den if-Ausdruck in when:


  fun translateNumber(n: Int): String {
    return when (n) {
      1 -> {
        "one"
      }
      2 -> {
        "two"
      }
      else -> {
        "???"
      }
    }
  }

Inspection ID: CascadeIf

Meldet Aufrufe des Pair-Konstruktors, die durch Aufrufe der Infix-Funktion to() ersetzt werden können.

Explizite Konstruktoraufrufe können redundant sein, insbesondere wenn sie mehrfach verwendet werden. Das Ersetzen von Konstruktoraufrufen durch to() verbessert die Lesbarkeit und Wartbarkeit des Codes.

Beispiel:


  val countries = mapOf(
      Pair("France", "Paris"),
      Pair("Spain", "Madrid"),
      Pair("Germany", "Berlin")
  )

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  val countries = mapOf(
      "France" to "Paris",
      "Spain" to "Madrid",
      "Germany" to "Berlin"
  )

Inspection ID: ConvertPairConstructorToToFunction

Meldet Funktionen, die einer der Operator-Konventionen entsprechen, denen jedoch das Schlüsselwort operator fehlt.

Durch Hinzufügen des Modifikators operator ermöglichen Sie es Benutzern der Funktion, idiomatischen Kotlin-Code zu schreiben.

Beispiel:


  class Complex(val real: Double, val imaginary: Double) {
      fun plus(other: Complex) =
          Complex(real + other.real, imaginary + other.imaginary)
  }

  fun usage(a: Complex, b: Complex) {
      a.plus(b)
  }

Der Quick-Fix fügt das Modifikator-Schlüsselwort operator hinzu:


  class Complex(val real: Double, val imaginary: Double) {
      operator fun plus(other: Complex) =
          Complex(real + other.real, imaginary + other.imaginary)
  }

  fun usage(a: Complex, b: Complex) {
      a + b
  }

Inspection ID: AddOperatorModifier

Meldet abschließende Kommas, die nicht dem empfohlenen Styleguide entsprechen.

Inspection ID: TrailingComma

inspection.displayName: Aufrufkette für Collections kann zur Verbesserung der Performance in 'Sequence' umgewandelt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Aufrufketten für eine Collection, die in eine Sequence konvertiert werden sollten.

Jede Collection-Transformationsfunktion (z. B. map() oder filter()) erstellt intern eine neue Collection (normalerweise eine List oder ein Set). Bei mehreren aufeinanderfolgenden Aufrufen und einer großen Anzahl von Einträgen in der Collection kann die Speicherbelastung erheblich sein. In solchen Fällen ist es besser, eine Sequence zu verwenden.

Beispiel:


  class Entity(val key: String, val value: String)

  fun getValues(lines: List<String>) = lines
      .filter { it.isNotEmpty() }
      .map { it.split(',', limit = 2) }
      .filter { it.size == 2 }
      .map { Entity(it[0], it[1]) }

Der Quick-Fix umschließt die Aufrufkette mit asSequence() und toList():


  class Entity(val key: String, val value: String)

  fun getValues(lines: List<String>) = lines
      .asSequence()
      .filter { it.isNotEmpty() }
      .map { it.split(',', limit = 2) }
      .filter { it.size == 2 }
      .map { Entity(it[0], it[1]) }
      .toList()

Inspection ID: ConvertCallChainIntoSequence

Berichtet über Aufrufe der Funktion to, die durch die Infix-Notation ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung der Infix-Notation kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt to durch die Infix-Notation.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(a: Int, b: Int) {
      val pair = a.to(b)
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(a: Int, b: Int) {
      val pair = a to b
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceToWithInfixForm

inspection.displayName: Aufruf von 'toString' kann durch String-Template ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet toString-Funktionsaufrufe, die durch ein String-Template ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung von String-Templates macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt toString durch ein String-Template.

Beispiel:


  fun test(): String {
      val x = 1
      return x.toString()
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun test(): String {
      val x = 1
      return "$x"
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceToStringWithStringTemplate

inspection.displayName: Boolesche Assertion kann durch Gleichheits-Assertion ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Aufrufe von assertTrue() und assertFalse(), die durch Assert-Gleichheitsfunktionen ersetzt werden können.

assertEquals(), assertSame() und deren negative Gegenstücke (-Not-) können detailliertere Informationen über den Fehlschlag liefern.

Beispiel:

  assertTrue(a == b)

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:

  assertEquals(a, b)

Inspection ID: ReplaceAssertBooleanWithAssertEquality

Meldet Größenprüfungen von Collections/Array/String, die durch isNotEmpty() ersetzt werden sollten.

Die Verwendung von isNotEmpty() macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die Größenprüfung durch isNotEmpty().

Beispiel:


  fun foo() {
      val arrayOf = arrayOf(1, 2, 3)
      arrayOf.size > 0
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun foo() {
      val arrayOf = arrayOf(1, 2, 3)
      arrayOf.isNotEmpty()
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceSizeCheckWithIsNotEmpty

inspection.displayName: String-Verkettung, die in ein String-Template konvertiert werden kann

group.inspection:
Meldet String-Verkettungen, die in String-Templates umgewandelt werden können.

Die Verwendung von String-Templates wird empfohlen, da sie die Lesbarkeit des Codes verbessern.

Beispiel:


  fun example() {
      val capitals = mapOf("France" to "Paris", "Spain" to "Madrid")
      for ((country, capital) in capitals) {
          print(capital + " is a capital of " + country)
      }
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun example() {
      val capitals = mapOf("France" to "Paris", "Spain" to "Madrid")
      for ((country, capital) in capitals) {
          print("$capital is a capital of $country")
      }
  }

Inspection ID: ConvertToStringTemplate

inspection.displayName: Aufruf einer Bibliotheksfunktion kann vereinfacht werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Bibliotheksfunktionsaufrufe, die durch eine vereinfachte Form ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung der entsprechenden Funktionen macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt diese Funktionsaufrufe durch passendere Alternativen.

Beispiel:


  fun test(list: List<Any>) {
      list.filter { it is String }
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun test(list: List<Any>) {
      list.filterIsInstance<String>()
  }

Inspection ID: SimplifiableCall

Meldet Aufrufe von isEmpty, isBlank, isNotEmpty oder isNotBlank in if-Anweisungen, die zur Zuweisung von Standardwerten verwendet werden.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die if-Bedingung durch einen ifEmpty- oder ifBlank-Aufruf.

Beispiel:


  fun test(list: List<Int>): List<Int> {
      return if (list.isEmpty()) {
          println()
          foo()
      } else {
          list
      }
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun test(list: List<Int>): List<Int> {
      return list.ifEmpty {
          println()
          foo()
      }
  }

Diese Inspektion wird nur gemeldet, wenn die Kotlin-Sprachversion des Projekts oder Moduls 1.3 oder höher ist.

Inspection ID: ReplaceIsEmptyWithIfEmpty

inspection.displayName: Prüfung auf Größe Null kann durch 'isEmpty()' ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet size == 0-Prüfungen für Collections/Arrays/Strings, die durch isEmpty() ersetzt werden sollten.

Die Verwendung von isEmpty() macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die Prüfung der Größe durch isEmpty().

Beispiel:


  fun foo() {
      val arrayOf = arrayOf(1, 2, 3)
      arrayOf.size == 0
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo() {
      val arrayOf = arrayOf(1, 2, 3)
      arrayOf.isEmpty()
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceSizeZeroCheckWithIsEmpty

Meldet Aufrufe von MutableCollection.addAll / +=, bei denen das Argument ein Aufruf von Iterable.map/filter ist.

Dieser Code alloziert ein zusätzliches List-Objekt, was durch die Verwendung von mapTo/filterTo vermieden werden kann.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den Aufruf durch mapTo/filterTo.

Beispiel:


    coll1.addAll(coll2.map { transform(it) })
    coll1 += coll2.map { transform(it) }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


    coll2.mapTo(coll1) { transform(it) }

Inspection ID: ReplaceAddAllWithMapTo

Meldet Aufrufe wie s.substring(0, s.length - x), die durch s.dropLast(x) ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung der entsprechenden Funktion macht den Code einfacher.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den substring-Aufruf durch dropLast.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(s: String) {
      s.substring(0, s.length - 5)
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(s: String) {
      s.dropLast(5)
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceSubstringWithDropLast

Detects properties with backing fields and suggests a refactoring introducing backing fields explicitly using a field keyword.

Example:


  private val _items = mutableListOf()
  val items: List get() = _items

After the quick-fix is applied:


  val items: List
    field = mutableListOf()

Inspection ID: ConvertToExplicitBackingFields

inspection.displayName: Gleichheitsprüfung kann anstelle des Elvis-Operators für Nullable-Boolean-Prüfungen verwendet werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Fälle, in denen eine Gleichheitsprüfung anstelle des Elvis-Operators verwendet werden sollte.

Beispiel:


fun check(a: Boolean? = null) {
    if (a ?: false) throw IllegalStateException()
}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


fun check(a: Boolean? = null) {
    if (a == true) throw IllegalStateException()
}

Inspection ID: NullableBooleanElvis

inspection.displayName: Verschachteltes Lambda hat einen überdeckten impliziten Parameter

group.inspection:
Meldet geschachtelte Lambdas mit überschatteten impliziten Parametern.

Beispiel:


fun foo(listOfLists: List<List<String>>) {
  listOfLists.forEach {
    it.forEach {
      println(it)
    }
  }
}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


fun foo(listOfLists: List<List<String>>) {
  listOfLists.forEach {
    it.forEach { it1 ->
      println(it1)
    }
  }
}

Inspection ID: NestedLambdaShadowedImplicitParameter

inspection.displayName: Java-Methoden sollten durch Kotlin-Entsprechungen ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet Java-Methodenaufrufe, wie z. B. System.out.println(), die durch Kotlin-Funktionen ersetzt werden können.

Das Ersetzen dieses Codes entfernt Java-Abhängigkeiten und macht den Kotlin-Code idiomatischer.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den Java-Methodenaufruf durch den entsprechenden Kotlin-Aufruf.

Beispiel:


  import java.util.Arrays

  fun main() {
      val a = Arrays.asList(1, 3, null)
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun main() {
      val a = listOf(1, 3, null)
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceJavaStaticMethodWithKotlinAnalog

Meldet return-Ausdrücke (Einzeiler oder when), die durch die Ausdruckskörper-Syntax ersetzt werden können.

Die Verwendung der Ausdruckskörper-Syntax wird im Styleguide empfohlen.

Der Quick-Fix In Ausdruckskörper umwandeln kann verwendet werden, um den Code automatisch zu korrigieren.

Beispiel:


  fun sign(x: Int): Int {
      return when { // <== kann vereinfacht werden
          x < 0 -> -1
          x > 0 -> 1
          else -> 0
      }
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun sign(x: Int): Int = when {
      x < 0 -> -1
      x > 0 -> 1
      else -> 0
  }

Inspection ID: UseExpressionBody

Meldet Aufrufe des Operators rangeTo oder .. anstelle von until.

Die Verwendung der entsprechenden Funktion kann den Code vereinfachen.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt den Aufruf von rangeTo oder .. durch until.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(a: Int) {
      for (i in 0..a - 1) {

      }
  }

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  fun foo(a: Int) {
      for (i in 0 until a) {

      }
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceRangeToWithUntil

inspection.displayName: Vollständig qualifizierter Name kann durch einen vorhandenen Import-Alias ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet vollqualifizierte Namen, die durch einen existierenden Import-Alias ersetzt werden können.

Beispiel:


import foo.Foo as Bar
fun main() {
    foo.Foo()
}

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


import foo.Foo as Bar
fun main() {
    Bar()
}

Inspection ID: ReplaceWithImportAlias

Berichtet über die Verwendung von implizitem this.

Beispiel:


  class Foo {
      fun s() = ""

      fun test() {
          s()
      }
  }

Der Quick-Fix gibt this explizit an:


  class Foo {
      fun s() = ""

      fun test() {
          this.s()
      }
  }

Inspection ID: ImplicitThis

Meldet arrayOf-Aufrufe, die durch ein Array-Literal [...] ersetzt werden können.

Beispiel:

  annotation class MyAnnotation(val strings: Array<String>)

  @MyAnnotation(arrayOf("alpha", "beta", "omega")) // Ersetzbares 'arrayOf()'
  class MyClass

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:

  annotation class MyAnnotation(val strings: Array<String>)

  @MyAnnotation(["alpha", "beta", "omega"])
  class MyClass

Inspection ID: ReplaceArrayOfWithLiteral

Meldet binäre Ausdrücke, die aus unterschiedlichen Operatoren ohne runde Klammern bestehen.

Solche Ausdrücke können aufgrund unterschiedlicher Operatorrangfolgeregeln schwer lesbar sein.

Beispiel:

  fun foo(b: Boolean?, i: Int?) {
      val x = b ?: i == null // wird als `(b ?: i) == null` ausgewertet
      val y = i ?: 0 + 1 // wird als `i ?: (0 + 1)` ausgewertet
  }

Inspection ID: UnclearPrecedenceOfBinaryExpression

inspection.displayName: Geboxte Eigenschaften sollten durch ungeboxte ersetzt werden

group.inspection:
Meldet geboxte Range.start- und Range.endInclusive-Eigenschaften.

Diese Eigenschaften können durch ungeboxte first- und last-Eigenschaften ersetzt werden, um redundante Aufrufe zu vermeiden.

Der Quick-Fix ersetzt die start- und endInclusive-Eigenschaften durch die entsprechenden first und last.

Beispiel:


  fun foo(range: CharRange) {
      val lastElement = range.endInclusive
  }

Nach der Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  fun foo(range: CharRange) {
      val lastElement = range.last
  }

Inspection ID: ReplaceRangeStartEndInclusiveWithFirstLast

group.name: TOML
group.inspection:
Meldet nicht aufgelöste Referenzen in TOML-Dateien.

Inspection ID: TomlUnresolvedReference

group.name: Android Lint: Play Policy
group.inspection:
Full Screen Intent Insights

On Android 14+, the USE_FULL_SCREEN_INTENT permission is auto-granted only for apps whose core function is setting alarms or handling calls. For any other use case, you must obtain explicit user consent and clearly explain your need. This policy prevents the misuse of full-screen intents for non-critical purposes and requires that your use does not interfere with or disrupt the user's device, other apps, or overall usability.

Dos:

- Request user consent for the permission and provide a clear explanation for the request if not auto-granted.
- Limit use to necessary high-priority notifications/alerts.
- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console to establish pre-grant eligibility for the full-screen intent permission if targeting Android 14+.

Don'ts:

- Use this permission for non-core or low-priority features.
- Use this permission to interfere with devices or other apps.
- Use this permission for disruptive ads or disruptive notifications.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-full-screen-intent
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-full-screen-intent
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: FullScreenIntentPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Advertising Id Insights

The Android advertising identifier (AAID) must only be used for advertising and user analytics; although we urge you to instead transition to App Set ID for analytics. You must respect users' opt-out selections and acquire explicit user consent if you link the AAID to personally identifiable information (PII). It is crucial to be transparent, so disclose any AAID collection and use in a legally adequate privacy policy.

Dos:

- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console for Google Play services permission.
- Use App Set ID for non-ads use cases (analytics, fraud).

Don'ts:

- Use persistent identifiers for advertising instead of AAID.
- Link AAID to PII for any analytics purposes -even with consent.
- Allow third-party code in your app to violate this policy.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-advertising-id
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-advertising-identifier
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: AdvertisingIdPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Accessibility Insights

Google Play permits the use of the AccessibilityService API across a wide range of applications. However, only services designed to help people with disabilities access their devices or overcome challenges due to their disabilities are eligible to declare themselves as accessibility tools by setting isAccessibilityTool=true in their metadata. These apps are exempt from the prominent disclosure and consent requirements. For all other uses, or if not declaring your app as an accessibility tool, you will be required to complete an accessibility declaration in Play Console and must implement a clear in-app disclosure explaining data access and use, and obtain affirmative user consent.

Dos:

- Declare isAccessibilityTool=true accurately if your app’s main goal is disability support in your service’s metadata file.
- Provide a clear Play Console declaration and demo video if using AccessibilityService API.
- Implement clear in-app disclosure and obtain user consent if not a designated accessibility tool.
- Complete an accessibility declaration when you submit a declaration form in your Play Console if you have not declared your app to be an accessibility tool but use the AccessibilityService API
- Limit data collection and use strictly to the disclosed and declared purposes.
- Use more narrowly scoped APIs and permissions in lieu of the Accessibility API when possible to achieve the desired functionality.

Don'ts:

- Misuse the Accessibility API to bypass privacy settings or alter UI without consent.
- Claim isAccessibilityTool=true if your app's core purpose is not supporting people with disabilities.
- Neglect the Play Console declaration, video, or necessary in-app disclosures.
- Collect data beyond what's essential for the declared accessibility features.
- Rely only on app descriptions or privacy policies for required user disclosures.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-accessibility
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-accessibility
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: AccessibilityPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

VPN Service Insights

The VpnService base class allows developers to create secure VPN solutions. Google Play permits its use only for apps with core VPN functionality or those requiring a remote server for essential features such as parental control, app usage tracking, device security, network tools, web browsers, or carrier services. It is paramount that VpnService is never used to collect personal or sensitive user data without prominent disclosure and explicit consent. Furthermore, redirecting or manipulating user traffic from other apps for monetization is strictly prohibited. All apps using VpnService must clearly document this in their Google Play listing and encrypt all data from the device to the VPN tunnel endpoint.

Dos:

- Document use of the VpnService clearly in the Google Play listing.
- Must encrypt the data from the device to the VPN tunnel end point.
- Ensure your app's core functionality aligns with VPN use or permitted exceptions.
- Provide prominent in-app disclosure and obtain explicit consent for any sensitive data collection.

Don'ts:

- Use VpnService for purposes other than core VPN or specified exceptions.
- Collect personal and sensitive user data without prominent disclosure and consent.
- Redirect or manipulate user traffic from other apps on a device for monetization purposes (for example, redirecting ads traffic through a country different than that of the user).

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-vpn
See developer guidance: https://goo.gle/dac-vpn
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: VpnServicePolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Package/App Visibility Insights

Accessing a user's installed app inventory is sensitive data. Google Play policy strictly limits broad visibility (QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES), allowing it only for core app functionality that requires extensive knowledge of installed apps for interoperability. You must prioritize using finite, targeted queries to access specific apps when possible, which can be more privacy-friendly. Under no circumstances can data from the installed app inventory be sold or shared for advertising or analytics monetization.

Dos:

- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console for QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES and any other high-risk permissions.
- For your app review, clearly document why your app needs app visibility whether broad or more targeted.
- Access only the minimum data needed.
- Follow the specific allowable use cases for QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES.

Don'ts:

- Request QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES if your need can be met with finite, targeted queries.
- Gain broad app visibility via methods not explicitly allowed by policy.
- Provide false information about your app's core functionality or data needs.
- Collect or use unnecessary data from installed app data.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-package-visibility
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-broad-package-visibility
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: PackageVisibilityPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Foreground Services Insights

For apps targeting Android 14+ you must declare valid Foreground Service (FGS) types in the manifest and Play Console, providing descriptions, user impact, and a demo video justifying their use based on user-initiated, perceptible actions.

Dos:

- Run FGS only for as long as necessary to complete the task.
- Ensure FGS provides a user-beneficial core app feature, is initiated by the user, is visible in notifications or is user perceptible (for example, audio from playing a song).
- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console if targeting Android 14+ and describe the use case for each Foreground Services (FGS) permission used. Ensure the appropriate FGS type is selected. Examples listed here.

Don'ts:

- Use FGS if system management of your task doesn’t break the user experience in your app. Consider alternatives like WorkManager
- Declare invalid or inaccurate FGS types in your app’s manifest.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-foreground-service
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-foreground-service
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: ForegroundServicesPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Health Connect Insights

Access to Health Connect data is restricted to apps with approved health, fitness, medical care, or health research core use cases. You must strictly limit data access to the minimum scope necessary for these approved functions and obtain explicit user consent before sharing any health data with third parties. Transparency is key, so provide clear disclosures and a comprehensive privacy policy explaining data collection, use, management, and deletion. Secure user data against unauthorized access and comply with all applicable laws and regulations (e.g., HIPAA, GDPR).

Dos:

- Your app must also comply with the Health apps policy if it qualifies as a health app or has health-related features and accesses health data including Health Connect data.
- See Health Connect policy requirement FAQs to request access to Health Connect data types and other FAQs.
- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console. For each permission requested, provide a clear and detailed justification explaining how your app will use the data to benefit the user.
- Request only the minimum necessary data types.
- Securely handle user data (e.g., use modern cryptography).

Don'ts:

- Use Health Connect in high-risk apps (e.g., aviation, control of life-critical systems such as pacemakers) or for children.
- Sell or transfer user data for advertising, creditworthiness, or data brokers.
- Use with medical devices without required regulatory compliance/clearances.
- Access Health Connect data for secondary or unapproved purposes.
- Request data permissions beyond your app's core functionality.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-health-connect
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-health-connect
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: HealthConnectPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

All Files Access Insights

Google Play policy treats access to user files and directories as sensitive and high risk access, so we restrict use of the MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permission on Android 11+. You must have essential core app functionality that requires broad access to this permission for a user-facing purpose, and never for third parties. This helps prevent unnecessary data collection and protects users' privacy. Apps requesting this permission must clearly prompt users so they can make an informed privacy decision, and get approval through Play’s app review.

Dos:

- Prioritize using privacy-friendly alternatives instead, like Storage Access Framework or MediaStore API.
- Declare this permissions when you submit a declaration form in your Play Console.
- Clearly define and document your app's core functionality in your app review.
- Clearly prompt users to enable “All files access” for your app under “Special app access” settings.

Don'ts:

- Request the MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permission for non-permitted use-cases like Media Files access or any File selection activity where the user manually selects individual files.
- Misrepresent the core functionality of your app.
- Store or share data beyond essential and disclosed needs.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-all-files-access
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-all-files-access
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: AllFilesAccessPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Request Install Packages Insights

REQUEST_INSTALL_PACKAGES permission allows apps to request the installation of other app packages. This permission is restricted to the app's core functionality, specifically when the primary purpose directly involves sending, receiving, or enabling user-initiated installation of app packages. Using this permission to update your app, change its functionality or bundle other APKs for silent or unauthorized installation (except enterprise management) is prohibited. All installations must be a direct, active choice by the user. Apps targeting Android 8+ must hold this permission in order to use Intent.ACTION_INSTALL_PACKAGE.

Dos:

- Clearly and prominently document the core functionality requiring this permission in your app’s Play Store description and when you submit a declaration form in your Play Console.
- Adhere strictly to the permitted functionalities including web browsing/search, file sharing/transfer/management, enterprise device management, backup/restore, device migration/phone transfer, companion app to sync phone to wearable or IoT device.
- Ensure your app prevents background or unintended installations. All app package installations must be explicitly initiated by the user.

Don'ts:

- Request this permission for a functionality that is not directly related to the primary purpose of your app. This includes Peer-to-Peer (P2P) sharing. P2P must be the primary purpose of the app in order to qualify as a permitted use.
- Request this permission when the required task can be done with a less intrusive method.
- Change how your app uses this permission without first revising your Play Console declaration with updated and accurate information. Deceptive and undeclared uses of this permission are prohibited.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-request-install-packages
See developer guidance: https://goo.gle/dac-request-install-packages
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: RequestInstallPackagesPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Exact Alarm Insights

USE_EXACT_ALARM permission on Android 13+ is a highly restricted permission used only for apps whose core, user-facing functionality genuinely requires precise timing, like dedicated alarm, timer, or calendar applications with event notifications. If your app does not have this specific core need, consider using SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM permission instead. It provides the same functionality but access must be granted by the user. This policy prevents misuse that impacts system resources.

Dos:

- Request the auto granted version of the permission, USE_EXACT_ALARM, only if your app’s core functionality is of alarm or calendar.
- Use SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM instead if the above criteria is not met.
- Complete Play Console declaration to indicate app functionality.

Don'ts:

- Use this permission for non-critical features that do not directly contribute to the app's main purpose.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/9888170
See developer guidance: https://developer.android.com/about/versions/13/features#use-exact-alarm-permission
See Declaration form: https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/9214102

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: ExactAlarmPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

SMS/Call Log Insights

Google Play imposes strict restrictions on accessing highly sensitive SMS and Call Log data. Your app must be the designated default handler for SMS, Phone, or Assistant to request these permissions. Usage is limited only to documented core app functionality that is absolutely essential for your app's primary purpose. This data must never be used for advertising or any other unapproved purpose.

Dos:

- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console.
- Clearly document the core functionality requiring access to your users.
- Use policy-compliant alternatives like the SMS Retriever API where possible.
- Stop accessing data immediately upon losing default handler status.

Don'ts:

- Request SMS/Call Log permissions without a core need justification.
- Use this data for advertising or purposes.
- Store or share unnecessary SMS or Call Log data.
- Attempt to derive this data using alternative methods.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-sms-call-log
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-sms-call-log
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: SmsAndCallLogPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Photos & Video Insights

To protect user privacy and security, Google Play requires apps that request READ_MEDIA_IMAGES or READ_MEDIA_VIDEO permissions to show strong, legitimate core use cases for persistent or frequent access to photos and videos. If your app does not qualify for this access, you should remove the permissions. Instead, use a system picker like the more privacy-preserving Android Photo Picker for one-time or infrequent needs.

Dos:

- Submit a declaration form in your Play Console.
- Use a system picker such as the photo picker if you don’t need broad access
- Provide evidence to justify your use case during app review.
- Clearly explain to users why your app needs these permissions.

Don'ts:

- Collect more photo or video data than necessary.
- Attempt to bypass or manipulate user consent.
- Block or gate functionality if a user declines a non-critical permission. Instead, use a system picker to gracefully handle the file request.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-photo-video
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-photo-video
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: PhotoAndVideoPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Background Location Insights

To protect user privacy, the background location policy requires apps to provide a strong justification and obtain explicit user consent for access. Device location data is limited to essential functions that directly benefit the user and are central to the app's core purpose; it is never permitted solely for advertising or analytics. Minimize your requests, choosing lesser sensitive options like coarse location and foreground access whenever possible. Foreground Services access of device location must be user-initiated and temporary, while background is only for critical features.

Dos:

- Comply with the Designed for Families policy for apps targeting children.
- Review additional key considerations and requirements to submit your app for access to location in the background permissions.
- Complete the Console declaration for background location.

Don'ts:

- Use device location solely for advertising or analytics purposes.
- Access data after it's no longer needed.
- Request device location for apps directed at children.
- Sell device location.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-background-location
See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-background-location
See Declaration form: https://goo.gle/play-permission-decl-form

Always review the full policy in the Policy Center to ensure compliance.

Issue id: BackgroundLocationPolicy

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

Login Credentials Policy Insight

To comply with Google Play policies, you must provide the information we need to access every part of your app. This allows our team to review your app for compliance before it reaches users. You can submit these details in the Play Console. Your credentials must remain active and accessible regardless of our reviewer's location. If your app uses dynamic security like two-factor authentication (2FA), provide a bypass or a static alternative. If you use non-text methods like QR codes, provide a static URL. For content behind paywalls, ensure we have full, free access to review those features.

Dos:

- Review our e-learning course on Login Credentials before submitting your app. - Disable 2FA or provide a bypass. - Include clear instructions if your app requires users to set up their own password (for example, a PIN). - Link to a static URL if you are providing a QR code for login access. - Ensure credentials work throughout the entire review.

Don'ts:

- Use credentials that expire during review. - Submit login details in non-English languages. - Forget instructions for third-party sign-ins. - Require reviewers to pay for subscriptions.

Helpful Links:

See Policy page: https://goo.gle/play-policy-login-credentials See Help Center article: https://goo.gle/play-help-login-credentials

Issue id: LoginCredentials

Vendor: Play policy insights beta

group.name: TestNG
group.inspection:
Meldet ungültige Methodennamen, die an das Attribut dependsOnMethods der Annotation @Test übergeben wurden.

Ein Methodenname wird als ungültig betrachtet, wenn er nicht zu einer gültigen, zugreifbaren mit @Test annotierten Methode in der aktuellen Klasse oder einer ihrer Basisklassen aufgelöst werden kann.

Beispiel:


  public class SampleTest {
    @Test(dependsOnMethods = "testSpellignError")
    public void testSample() {}
    @Test
    public void testSpellingError(){}
  }

Inspection ID: dependsOnMethodTestNG

Meldet Methoden, die mit @DataProvider annotiert sind, aber weder Object[][] noch Iterator<Object> zurückgeben. Wenn ein anderer Typ zurückgegeben wird, löst TestNG eine Exception aus.

Beispiel:


public class TestNgTest {
  @DataProvider(name = "Languages")
  List<String> getData() {
    return List.of("Java", "Kotlin");
  }

  @Test(dataProvider = "Languages")
  public void testData(String language) {
    System.out.println(language);
  }
}

Inspection ID: DataProviderReturnType

Meldet nicht definierte Gruppennamen, die an die Attribute dependsOnGroups oder groups in der @Test-Annotation übergeben werden.

Der Quick-Fix fügt den nicht definierten Namen der Liste der bekannten Gruppen hinzu.

Verwenden Sie das Feld Definierte Gruppen, um eine kommagetrennte Liste bekannter Gruppen zu definieren.

Inspection ID: groupsTestNG

Meldet Testklassen, die nicht in testing.xml registriert sind. Dies ist normalerweise ein Fehler, da solche Tests nicht ausgeführt werden.

Inspection ID: UndeclaredTests

inspection.displayName: JUnit-Test kann in TestNG konvertiert werden

group.inspection:
Berichtet über JUnit-basierte Testklassen, die in TestNG-basierte Unit-Tests konvertiert werden können.

Beispiel:


import org.junit.Test;
import static org.junit.Assert.*;

public class ExampleTest {
  @Test
  public void testExample(){
    assertEquals(2 + 2, 4);
  }
}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


import org.testng.Assert;
import org.testng.annotations.Test;

public class ExampleTest {
  @Test
  public void testExample(){
    Assert.assertEquals(4, 2 + 2);
  }
}

Inspection ID: JUnitTestNG

inspection.displayName: TestNG Javadoc kann in Annotationen konvertiert werden

group.inspection:
Erkennt TestNG-Tests mit Javadoc-Annotationen und konvertiert diese in JDK-Annotationen.

Beispiel:


  /**
    * @testng.before-test
  */
  public void sample() {}

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fix:


  @BeforeTest
  public void sample() {}

Inspection ID: ConvertJavadoc

Meldet Verweise auf nicht existierende oder nicht zugängliche Data-Provider-Methoden.

Beispiel:


public class InstanceDataProviderFromForeignClass {
  // Methode data() existiert nicht in Klasse A
  @Test(dataProvider = "data", dataProviderClass = A.class)
  public void test() {
  }
}
class A { }

Nach Anwendung des Quickfixes:


// Die erforderliche data()-Methode wird in Klasse A erstellt
class A {
  @DataProvider
  public Object[][] data() {
    return new Object[][]{};
  }
}

Inspection ID: MalformedDataProvider

Meldet die TestNG-Annotation org.testng.annotations.Configuration.

Es wird empfohlen, moderne @BeforeXXX/@AfterXXX-Annotationen zur Konfiguration von Test-Suites zu verwenden.

Beispiel:


  public class Test {
    @Configuration(beforeSuite = true, afterTest = true)
    public void afterBefore(){
    }
  }

Nach Anwendung des Quick-Fixes:


  public class Test {
    @AfterTest()
    @BeforeSuite()
    public void afterBefore(){
    }
  }

Inspection ID: ConvertOldAnnotations

inspection.displayName: Erwartete Ausnahme wird im Testmethodenrumpf nie geworfen

group.inspection:
Meldet geprüfte Exceptions, die von einer TestNG-Testmethode erwartet, aber niemals innerhalb des Methodenkörpers geworfen werden.

Beispiel:


  @Test(expectedExceptions = Exception.class) // Warnung: Erwartete 'Exception' wird niemals geworfen
  public void testEngineIsRunning() {
    assertTrue(engine.isRunning());
  }

Inspection ID: ExpectedExceptionNeverThrownTestNG

Meldet TestNG-Data-Provider mit demselben Namen, die eine org.testng.TestNGException verursachen.

Beispiel:


  public class DuplicatedDataProviders {
    @DataProvider
    public Object[][] intTestData() { // Duplikat 1
      return new Integer[][]{
        new Integer[]{1, 1},
      };
    }

    @DataProvider(name = "intTestData")
    public Object[][] someTestData() { // Duplikat 2
      return new Integer[][]{
        new Integer[]{1, 1},
      };
    }

    @Test(dataProvider = "intTestData")
    public void testIsOK(Integer key, Integer value) {
      assertEquals(key, value);
    }
  }

Inspection ID: DuplicatedDataProviderNames

group.name: Nicht verwendeter Code
group.inspection:
Meldet Objective-C-Klassen und -Protokolle, die nicht verwendet werden.

Inspection ID: OCUnusedClass

Meldet Methoden, die niemals aufgerufen werden, zum Beispiel in Message-Send-Ausdrücken, @selector-Ausdrücken sowie an anderen Stellen wie XIB-Dateien.

Inspection ID: OCUnusedMethod

Meldet Eigenschaften, auf die weder in qualifizierten Ausdrücken noch in Accessor-Methodenaufrufen zugegriffen oder geschrieben wird.

Inspection ID: OCUnusedProperty

Meldet Instanzvariablen, auf die weder zugegriffen wird noch die beschrieben werden.

Inspection ID: OCUnusedInstanceVariable

group.name: Android Lint: Security
group.inspection:
addJavascriptInterface Called

For applications built for API levels below 17, WebView#addJavascriptInterface presents a security hazard as JavaScript on the target web page has the ability to use reflection to access the injected object's public fields and thus manipulate the host application in unintended ways.

Issue id: AddJavascriptInterface

https://developer.android.com/reference/android/webkit/WebView.html#addJavascriptInterface(java.lang.Object,%20java.lang.String)
https://support.google.com/faqs/answer/9095419?hl=en
https://goo.gle/AddJavascriptInterface

Hardware Id Usage

Using these device identifiers is not recommended other than for high value fraud prevention and advanced telephony use-cases. For advertising use-cases, use AdvertisingIdClient$Info#getId and for analytics, use InstanceId#getId.

Issue id: HardwareIds

https://developer.android.com/training/articles/user-data-ids.html

Missing @JavascriptInterface on methods

As of API 17, you must annotate methods in objects registered with the addJavascriptInterface method with a @JavascriptInterface annotation.

Issue id: JavascriptInterface

https://developer.android.com/reference/android/webkit/WebView.html#addJavascriptInterface(java.lang.Object, java.lang.String)
https://goo.gle/JavascriptInterface

Using BC Provider

The BC provider has been deprecated and will not be provided when targetSdkVersion is P or higher.

Issue id: DeprecatedProvider

https://android-developers.googleblog.com/2018/03/cryptography-changes-in-android-p.html
https://goo.gle/DeprecatedProvider

Bidirectional text spoofing

Unicode bidirectional text characters can alter the order in which the compiler processes tokens. However, this can also be used to hide malicious code, and can be difficult to spot. This lint check audits the source code and looks for cases where it looks like bidirectional text has the potential to be misleading.

Issue id: BidiSpoofing

https://krebsonsecurity.com/2021/11/trojan-source-bug-threatens-the-security-of-all-code/
https://goo.gle/BidiSpoofing

Missing data extraction rules

Before Android 12, the attributes android:allowBackup and android:fullBackupContent were used to configure all forms of backup, including cloud backups, device-to-device transfers and adb backup.

In Android 12 and higher, these attributes have been deprecated and will only apply to cloud backups. You should instead use the attribute android:dataExtractionRules, specifying an @xml resource that configures which files to back up, for cloud backups and for device-to-device transfers, separately. If your minSdkVersion supports older versions, you'll still want to specify an android:fullBackupContent resource if the default behavior is not right for your app.

Issue id: DataExtractionRules

https://developer.android.com/about/versions/12/backup-restore#xml-changes
https://goo.gle/DataExtractionRules

TileProvider does not set permission

TileProviders should require the com.google.android.wearable.permission.BIND_TILE_PROVIDER permission to prevent arbitrary apps from binding to it.

Issue id: TileProviderPermissions

inspection.displayName: Implicit intent matches an internal non-exported component

group.inspection:
Implicit intent matches an internal non-exported component

This intent matches a non-exported component within the same app. In many cases, the app developer could instead use an explicit Intent to send messages to their internal components, ensuring that the messages are safely delivered without exposure to malicious apps on the device. Using such implicit intents will result in a crash in an upcoming version of Android.

Issue id: UnsafeImplicitIntentLaunch

inspection.displayName: Incorrect usage of getCallingUid() or getCallingPid()

group.inspection:
Incorrect usage of getCallingUid() or getCallingPid()

Binder.getCallingUid() and Binder.getCallingPid() will return information about the current process if called inside a thread that is not handling a binder transaction. This can cause security issues. If you still want to use your own uid/pid, use Process.myUid() or Process.myPid().

Issue id: BinderGetCallingInMainThread

inspection.displayName: Using a fixed seed with SecureRandom

group.inspection:
Using a fixed seed with SecureRandom

Specifying a fixed seed will cause the instance to return a predictable sequence of numbers. This may be useful for testing but it is not appropriate for secure use.

Issue id: SecureRandom

https://goo.gle/SecureRandom
https://developer.android.com/reference/java/security/SecureRandom.html

Insecure HostnameVerifier

This check looks for use of HostnameVerifier implementations whose verify method always returns true (thus trusting any hostname) which could result in insecure network traffic caused by trusting arbitrary hostnames in TLS/SSL certificates presented by peers.

Issue id: AllowAllHostnameVerifier

https://goo.gle/AllowAllHostnameVerifier

inspection.displayName: Proceeds with the HTTPS connection despite SSL errors

group.inspection:
Proceeds with the HTTPS connection despite SSL errors

This check looks for onReceivedSslError implementations that invoke SslErrorHandler#proceed.

Issue id: WebViewClientOnReceivedSslError

https://goo.gle/WebViewClientOnReceivedSslError

inspection.displayName: Value specified for permission is a known error

group.inspection:
Value specified for permission is a known error

This check looks for values specified in component permissions that are known errors, such as android:permission="true".

Please double check the permission value you have supplied. The value is expected to be a permission string from the system, another app, or your own, NOT a boolean.

Issue id: KnownPermissionError

https://goo.gle/KnownPermissionError

inspection.displayName: Permission appears to be a standard permission with a typo

group.inspection:
Permission appears to be a standard permission with a typo

This check looks for required permissions that look like well-known system permissions or permissions from the Android SDK, but aren't, and may be typos.

Please double check the permission value you have supplied.

Issue id: SystemPermissionTypo

Packaged private key

In general, you should not package private key files inside your app.

Issue id: PackagedPrivateKey

https://goo.gle/PackagedPrivateKey

Mutable Implicit PendingIntent is disallowed

Apps targeting Android 14 and above are not allowed to create PendingIntents with FLAG_MUTABLE and an implicit intent within for security reasons.

To retrieve an existing PendingIntent, use FLAG_NO_CREATE. To create a new PendingIntent, either make the intent explicit, or make it immutable with FLAG_IMMUTABLE.

Issue id: MutableImplicitPendingIntent

Using C2DM

The C2DM library does not work on Android P or newer devices; you should migrate to Firebase Cloud Messaging to ensure reliable message delivery.

Issue id: UsingC2DM

https://developers.google.com/cloud-messaging/c2dm

Native code outside library directory

In general, application native code should only be placed in the application's library directory, not in other locations such as the res or assets directories. Placing the code in the library directory provides increased assurance that the code will not be tampered with after application installation. Application developers should use the features of their development environment to place application native libraries into the lib directory of their compiled APKs. Embedding non-shared library native executables into applications should be avoided when possible.

Issue id: UnsafeNativeCodeLocation

inspection.displayName: Trusting ContentProvider filenames without any sanitization

group.inspection:
Trusting ContentProvider filenames without any sanitization

When communicating between applications with files, the server app can provide the client app with a maliciously constructed filename. The client app should never trust this filename and should either sanitize it or completely discard it.

Issue id: UnsanitizedFilenameFromContentProvider

https://developer.android.com/privacy-and-security/risks/untrustworthy-contentprovider-provided-filename

Insecure HostnameVerifier

This check looks for implementations of HostnameVerifier whose verify method always returns true (thus trusting any hostname) which could result in insecure network traffic caused by trusting arbitrary hostnames in TLS/SSL certificates presented by peers.

Issue id: BadHostnameVerifier

https://goo.gle/BadHostnameVerifier

inspection.displayName: File.setWritable() used to make file world-writable

group.inspection:
File.setWritable() used to make file world-writable

Setting files world-writable is very dangerous, and likely to cause security holes in applications. It is strongly discouraged; instead, applications should use more formal mechanisms for interactions such as ContentProvider, BroadcastReceiver, and Service.

Issue id: SetWorldWritable

https://goo.gle/SetWorldWritable

Allowing User Certificates

Allowing user certificates could allow eavesdroppers to intercept data sent by your app, which could impact the privacy of your users. Consider nesting your app's trust-anchors inside a <debug-overrides> element to make sure they are only available when android:debuggable is set to true.

Issue id: AcceptsUserCertificates

https://goo.gle/AcceptsUserCertificates
https://developer.android.com/training/articles/security-config#TrustingDebugCa

openFileOutput() with MODE_WORLD_WRITEABLE

There are cases where it is appropriate for an application to write world writeable files, but these should be reviewed carefully to ensure that they contain no private data, and that if the file is modified by a malicious application it does not trick or compromise your application.

Issue id: WorldWriteableFiles

https://goo.gle/WorldWriteableFiles

inspection.displayName: Library is marked as deprecated in SDK Index

group.inspection:
Library is marked as deprecated in SDK Index

This library has been deprecated, please consider updating to an alternative SDK before publishing a new release.

Issue id: PlaySdkIndexDeprecated

https://developer.android.com/distribute/sdk-index

Cipher.getInstance with ECB

Cipher#getInstance should not be called with ECB as the cipher mode or without setting the cipher mode because the default mode on android is ECB, which is insecure.

Issue id: GetInstance

https://goo.gle/GetInstance

Invalid Permission Attribute

Not all elements support the permission attribute. If a permission is set on an invalid element, it is a no-op and ignored. Ensure that this permission attribute was set on the correct element to protect the correct component.

Issue id: InvalidPermission

inspection.displayName: Hardcoded value of android:debuggable in the manifest

group.inspection:
Hardcoded value of android:debuggable in the manifest

It's best to leave out the android:debuggable attribute from the manifest. If you do, then the tools will automatically insert android:debuggable=true when building an APK to debug on an emulator or device. And when you perform a release build, such as Exporting APK, it will automatically set it to false.

If on the other hand you specify a specific value in the manifest file, then the tools will always use it. This can lead to accidentally publishing your app with debug information.

Issue id: HardcodedDebugMode

https://goo.gle/HardcodedDebugMode

inspection.displayName: Libraries with Privacy or Security Risks

group.inspection:
Libraries with Privacy or Security Risks

Your app is using a version of a library that has been identified by the library developer as a potential source of privacy and/or security risks. This may be a violation of Google Play policies (see https://play.google.com/about/monetization-ads/ads/) and/or affect your app’s visibility on the Play Store.

When available, the individual error messages from lint will include details about the reasons for this advisory.

Please try updating your app with an updated version of this library, or remove it from your app.

Issue id: RiskyLibrary

https://developer.android.com/distribute/sdk-index
https://goo.gle/RiskyLibrary

Using Deleted Provider

The Crypto provider has been completely removed in Android P (and was deprecated in an earlier release). This means that the code will throw a NoSuchProviderException and the app will crash. Even if the code catches that exception at a higher level, this is not secure and should not be used.

Issue id: DeletedProvider

https://android-developers.googleblog.com/2018/03/cryptography-changes-in-android-p.html
https://goo.gle/DeletedProvider

Unprotected SMS BroadcastReceiver

BroadcastReceivers that declare an intent-filter for SMS_DELIVER or SMS_RECEIVED must ensure that the caller has the BROADCAST_SMS permission, otherwise it is possible for malicious actors to spoof intents.

Issue id: UnprotectedSMSBroadcastReceiver

https://goo.gle/UnprotectedSMSBroadcastReceiver

Insecure Base Configuration

Permitting cleartext traffic could allow eavesdroppers to intercept data sent by your app, which impacts the privacy of your users. Consider only allowing encrypted traffic by setting the cleartextTrafficPermitted tag to false.

Issue id: InsecureBaseConfiguration

https://goo.gle/InsecureBaseConfiguration
https://developer.android.com/preview/features/security-config.html

inspection.displayName: Exported service does not require permission

group.inspection:
Exported service does not require permission

Exported services (services which either set exported=true or contain an intent-filter and do not specify exported=false) should define a permission that an entity must have in order to launch the service or bind to it. Without this, any application can use this service.

Issue id: ExportedService

https://goo.gle/ExportedService

Implements custom TLS trust manager

This check looks for custom X509TrustManager implementations.

Issue id: CustomX509TrustManager

https://goo.gle/CustomX509TrustManager

Call to SSLCertificateSocketFactory.getInsecure()

The SSLCertificateSocketFactory.getInsecure() method returns an SSLSocketFactory with all TLS/SSL security checks disabled, which could result in insecure network traffic caused by trusting arbitrary TLS/SSL certificates presented by peers. This method should be avoided unless needed for a special circumstance such as debugging. Instead, SSLCertificateSocketFactory.getDefault() should be used.

Issue id: SSLCertificateSocketFactoryGetInsecure

https://goo.gle/SSLCertificateSocketFactoryGetInsecure

inspection.displayName: Unsafe intent launched with no action set

group.inspection:
Unsafe intent launched with no action set

Intents that have no action and do not specify a component are a potential security risk, and using them will result in a crash in an upcoming version of Android. If a specific app is being targeted (including the case where the current app is the target) then set the targeted component using setComponent(), setClass(), setClassName(), or the Intent constructors that take a Class parameter. If the intent is not intended for a specific app then the action name should be set.

Issue id: IntentWithNullActionLaunch

inspection.displayName: Permission name does not follow recommended convention

group.inspection:
Permission name does not follow recommended convention

Permissions should be prefixed with an app's package name, using reverse-domain-style naming. This prefix should be followed by .permission., and then a description of the capability that the permission represents, in upper SNAKE_CASE. For example, com.example.myapp.permission.ENGAGE_HYPERSPACE.

Following this recommendation avoids naming collisions, and helps clearly identify the owner and intention of a custom permission.

Issue id: PermissionNamingConvention

Receiver does not require permission

Exported receivers (receivers which either set exported=true or contain an intent-filter and do not specify exported=false) should define a permission that an entity must have in order to launch the receiver or bind to it. Without this, any application can use this receiver.

Issue id: ExportedReceiver

https://goo.gle/ExportedReceiver

Using setJavaScriptEnabled

Your code should not invoke setJavaScriptEnabled if you are not sure that your app really requires JavaScript support.

Issue id: SetJavaScriptEnabled

https://goo.gle/SetJavaScriptEnabled
https://developer.android.com/training/articles/security-tips

Unspecified android:exported in manifest

Apps targeting Android 12 and higher are required to specify an explicit value for android:exported when the corresponding component has an intent filter defined. Otherwise, installation will fail. Set it to true to make this activity accessible to other apps, and false to limit it to be used only by this app or the OS. For launch activities, this should be set to true; otherwise, the app will fail to launch.

Previously, android:exported for components without any intent filters present used to default to false, and when intent filters were present, the default was true. Defaults which change value based on other values are confusing and lead to apps accidentally exporting components as a side-effect of adding intent filters. This is a security risk, and we have made this change to avoid introducing accidental vulnerabilities.

While the default without intent filters remains unchanged, it is now required to explicitly specify a value when intent filters are present. Any app failing to meet this requirement will fail to install on any Android version after Android 11.

We recommend setting android:exported to false (even on previous versions of Android prior to this requirement) unless you have a good reason to export a particular component.

Issue id: IntentFilterExportedReceiver

https://goo.gle/IntentFilterExportedReceiver

PreferenceActivity should not be exported

Fragment injection gives anyone who can send your PreferenceActivity an intent the ability to load any fragment, with any arguments, in your process.

Issue id: ExportedPreferenceActivity

http://securityintelligence.com/new-vulnerability-android-framework-fragment-injection
https://goo.gle/ExportedPreferenceActivity

inspection.displayName: Provider with readPermission only and implemented write APIs

group.inspection:
Provider with readPermission only and implemented write APIs

This check looks for Content Providers that only have the readPermission attribute but implement write APIs.

If android:readPermission is specified and both android:permission and android:writePermission are omitted, other apps can access any write operations that this provider exposes with no permission check. For a quick fix, changing the existing android:readPermission to android:permission will protect both read and write access with the same permission. Alternatively, declaring a separate android:writePermission can protect write access with a different permission.
